subtopic: Normal labour - duration of second stage in nulliparous woman with epidural
stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 39+5 in spontaneous labour has progressed to full dilatation with a working epidural in situ and has been pushing actively for 90 minutes. The fetal head is at +1 station in direct OA, with mild caput but no moulding. The CTG is normal with baseline 140, variability 10-15, accelerations present and no decelerations. Contractions are 4 in 10 lasting 60 seconds, palpating moderate to strong. The liquor remains clear. She has no medical comorbidities and the 36-week growth scan was unremarkable. The midwife asks the resident at what point a delay in the active second stage should be diagnosed in her circumstances according to RANZCOG and NICE definitions.

data_table.Examination: Full dilatation, +1 station, direct OA, mild caput, no moulding.
data_table.CTG: Baseline 140 bpm, variability 10-15, accelerations present, no decelerations.
data_table.Contractions: 4 in 10, 60 seconds, palpate moderate to strong.

lead_in: After how long of active pushing is a delayed second stage diagnosed in a nulliparous woman with a working epidural?

options.text: After 1 hour of active pushing
options.rationale: 1 hour is too short to define delay in a nullipara with an epidural; the active second stage threshold for delay is 2 hours without an epidural or 3 hours with one (RANZCOG; NICE NG235).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: After 2 hours of active pushing
options.rationale: 2 hours defines delay in a nulliparous woman without an epidural; with an epidural the threshold is extended to 3 hours of active pushing (RANZCOG; NICE NG235).

options.text: After 3 hours of active pushing
options.rationale: In a nulliparous woman with a working epidural, delay in the active second stage is diagnosed after 3 hours of active pushing without imminent birth, prompting review for assisted delivery or caesarean. Without an epidural the threshold is 2 hours; in multiparas it is 2 hours with epidural and 1 hour without (RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017; NICE NG235).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: After 4 hours of active pushing
options.rationale: 4 hours exceeds any guideline threshold and risks maternal exhaustion, fetal acidosis and increased operative morbidity; review is mandated by 3 hours in a nullipara with an epidural (RANZCOG; NICE NG235).

options.text: Only once the head fails to descend below the ischial spines regardless of time
options.rationale: Descent is one component of progress, but RANZCOG and NICE define delay in active second stage by elapsed time of active pushing rather than station alone (RANZCOG; NICE NG235).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

explanation.summary: Active second stage delay thresholds: nullipara without epidural 2 h, with epidural 3 h; multipara without epidural 1 h, with epidural 2 h. The passive second stage (from full dilatation to active pushing) is allowed up to 2 h in nullipara, 1 h in multipara to allow descent.
key_points: Stage 1: latent (<4 cm) and active (4-10 cm); active progress benchmark ~0.5-1 cm/h in nullipara
Stage 2: passive (full dilatation to active pushing) + active (pushing)
Stage 3: delivery of baby to delivery of placenta; active management <=30 min
Epidural extends acceptable second stage duration by 1 h
Multiparas progress faster in all stages
explanation.pearls: If you cannot remember the four numbers, anchor on nullipara-no-epidural = 2 h and add 1 h for epidural, subtract 1 h for multipara.

sources.label: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017
KEMH Clinical Guidelines - Labour and Birth

subtopic: Bishop score and selection of induction agent in an unfavourable cervix
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 41+2 with an uncomplicated singleton pregnancy attends the day assessment unit for induction of labour for post-dates. Her previous birth was a spontaneous vaginal delivery at 39+4 weeks with a normal third stage and no perineal trauma beyond a small first-degree tear. She has no medical comorbidities, no allergies, no uterine surgery and no previous caesarean. The fetus is cephalic with a reassuring CTG today, an estimated fetal weight on the 60th centile at her 36-week scan and normal umbilical and middle cerebral artery Dopplers at that time. Liquor volume is normal. Membranes are intact and there has been no PV bleeding, no PV loss and no offensive odour. On vaginal examination the cervix is 1 cm dilated, 1 cm long, firm in consistency, posterior in position, and the presenting part is at -3 station, giving a Bishop score of 3. She has not had any contractions and reports normal fetal movements. She is keen to maximise her chance of vaginal birth and to remain as ambulant as possible, and she asks about the safest first agent to use to begin induction at her cervical state.

data_table.Examination: Cervix: 1 cm dilated, 1 cm long, firm consistency, posterior position. Station -3. Cephalic, membranes intact.
data_table.Bishop score: Dilatation 1 (1), length 1 cm (2), consistency firm (0), position posterior (0), station -3 (0) = 3.
data_table.Past obstetric: One previous SVD at 39+4, no perineal trauma, no PPH, no instrumental.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial induction method?

options.text: Artificial rupture of membranes followed by oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: ARM is not feasible with a closed cervix at -3 station and an unfavourable Bishop score of 3; cervical ripening is required first (RANZCOG IOL 2021; KEMH).

options.text: Mechanical cervical ripening with a transcervical Foley catheter
options.rationale: A Bishop score of 3 indicates an unfavourable cervix and a mechanical Foley balloon is an appropriate first-line ripening agent, with comparable efficacy to prostaglandins but lower rates of hyperstimulation and CTG abnormalities. PGE2 is also acceptable; in this multipara with intact membranes Foley is widely preferred in Australian units to reduce hyperstimulation risk and allow outpatient ripening when local protocols permit (RANZCOG Induction of Labour 2021; Cochrane mechanical methods for IOL 2019; KEMH IOL guideline).

options.text: Oxytocin infusion commenced at 2 mU/min and titrated to contractions
options.rationale: Oxytocin alone with an unripe closed cervix is ineffective and associated with prolonged induction, exhaustion and increased caesarean; ripening must precede oxytocin (RANZCOG IOL 2021).

options.text: Misoprostol 200 microg oral every 2 hours until contractions established
options.rationale: Oral misoprostol dosing for IOL is 25-50 microg every 2-4 h in Australian protocols; 200 microg is a dose used for postpartum haemorrhage or termination of pregnancy and carries a high risk of hyperstimulation if given for IOL (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).

options.text: Stretch and sweep at the bedside and discharge home for review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Sweep alone is unlikely to initiate labour with a Bishop of 3 at 41+2, and post-dates beyond 41+0 warrants formal induction in Australian practice rather than expectant management to 42+0 (RANZCOG IOL 2021).

explanation.summary: Bishop score guides IOL method: <=6 = unfavourable, ripening needed (Foley balloon or PGE2); >=7 = favourable, proceed to ARM and oxytocin. Mechanical Foley and prostaglandins are equivalent for vaginal delivery rates with less hyperstimulation with Foley.
key_points: Bishop components: Dilatation, Effacement (length), Consistency, Position, Station
Foley balloon: 30-60 mL, left in situ up to 24 h, no fetal monitoring needed during ripening (outpatient option in many AU units)
PGE2 gel/pessary (Cervidil/Prostin): inpatient with CTG, risk of hyperstimulation
Misoprostol 25-50 microg orally or vaginally for IOL
Previous CS: Foley preferred; PGE2 and misoprostol contraindicated (rupture risk)
explanation.pearls: If the cervix is closed and posterior, you cannot ARM. Ripen first. Bishop <=6 means ripen, >=7 means ARM/oxytocin.

subtopic: Indications for induction of labour - post-dates threshold in Australian practice
stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 41+0 with an uncomplicated singleton pregnancy attends antenatal clinic having declined an IOL discussion at her 40-week appointment. EDD has been confirmed by an 8-week dating scan. The fetus is cephalic, growth-appropriate on the 36-week scan (50th centile, normal Dopplers, normal AFI), the CTG today is reassuring and the cervix is 2 cm dilated, 50% effaced, soft, mid-position with station -2, giving a Bishop score of 6. She feels well, fetal movements are normal, BP is 118/74 and urinalysis is negative. She is uncertain whether to consent to IOL and asks specifically what the strongest evidence-based reason is to recommend induction now rather than awaiting spontaneous labour to 42+0.

data_table.Examination: Bishop 6. SFH 38 cm at 41+0. Reassuring CTG.
data_table.Investigations: 8-week dating scan confirms gestation. 36-week growth scan: 50th centile, normal AFI, normal Dopplers.

lead_in: Which best describes the evidence-based rationale for recommending induction of labour at 41+0 in this woman?

options.text: Induction at 41+0 reduces perinatal mortality and meconium aspiration compared with expectant management to 42+0
options.rationale: The SWEPIS trial (BMJ 2019) was stopped early for perinatal mortality benefit, and Cochrane 2020 meta-analysis demonstrates that induction at or shortly after 41+0 reduces perinatal mortality, stillbirth and meconium aspiration syndrome compared with expectant management to 42+0, without an increase in caesarean. RANZCOG recommends offering IOL between 41+0 and 42+0 to all low-risk women (RANZCOG Induction of Labour 2021; Cochrane 2020; SWEPIS 2019).
source_refs: Cochrane Induction of labour at or beyond 37 weeks 2020

options.text: Induction at 41+0 reduces caesarean section rates compared with expectant management
options.rationale: Meta-analyses show induction at 41+0 does not increase and may modestly reduce caesarean, but the primary driver for recommendation is perinatal mortality reduction, not caesarean rates (Cochrane 2020).
source_refs: Cochrane Induction of labour at or beyond 37 weeks 2020

options.text: Induction at 41+0 is recommended to reduce maternal anxiety and antenatal clinic attendances
options.rationale: Maternal preference and convenience are not the evidence base; the recommendation is driven by reduction in stillbirth and perinatal mortality (RANZCOG).

options.text: Induction at 41+0 is recommended because placental insufficiency is invariable after 41 weeks
options.rationale: Placental insufficiency is not invariable; the rationale is a population-level rise in stillbirth from approximately 0.5 to 3 per 1000 between 40 and 42 weeks rather than universal placental failure (RANZCOG).

options.text: Induction at 41+0 is recommended only when oligohydramnios or abnormal Dopplers are present
options.rationale: Post-dates IOL is offered to all low-risk women between 41+0 and 42+0 regardless of liquor or Dopplers; abnormal surveillance would prompt earlier delivery (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Australian practice offers IOL between 41+0 and 42+0 for low-risk women to reduce stillbirth and perinatal mortality. Other common indications: term PROM (after 18-24 h), GDM at 38-40 weeks depending on control, pre-eclampsia >=37 weeks, FGR with adverse surveillance, maternal request after 39+0.
key_points: Stillbirth risk: ~0.5/1000 at 40 weeks, ~1/1000 at 41 weeks, ~3/1000 at 42 weeks
SWEPIS 2019 stopped early for perinatal mortality benefit of IOL at 41+0
GDM diet-controlled: deliver by 40+6; on metformin/insulin: 38+0 to 39+6
PE >=37+0: deliver
PROM at term: IOL within 24 h reduces chorioamnionitis (Hannah TermPROM)
explanation.pearls: The reason 41+0 IOL exists is stillbirth, not convenience and not CS rates.

sources.label: Cochrane Induction of labour at or beyond 37 weeks 2020

subtopic: CTG terminology - distinguishing variable from late decelerations
stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 40+1 in spontaneous labour at 6 cm has a continuous CTG sited for thick meconium-stained liquor at ARM 90 minutes ago. She has no medical comorbidities, the fetus is appropriately grown with normal Dopplers, and oxytocin has not been used. The fetus is cephalic, head at -1 station, direct OA. Contractions are 4 in 10 lasting 50 seconds, palpating moderate with normal resting tone. Maternal observations are stable. Over the last 30 minutes the CTG shows a baseline of 135 bpm, variability of 10 to 15 bpm, occasional accelerations and recurrent decelerations occurring with most contractions. The decelerations have a sharp drop from the baseline beginning at the start of the contraction, reach a nadir of 90 bpm within 20 seconds, last around 40 seconds, often have a small acceleration immediately before the drop and a small overshoot before returning to baseline, and recover by the end of the contraction. There are no decelerations between contractions.

data_table.CTG features: Baseline 135, variability 10-15, accelerations occasional, recurrent decelerations with sharp drop, nadir within 20 sec, shoulders present, return to baseline by end of contraction.

lead_in: Which type of deceleration is most consistent with these features and what is the underlying mechanism?

options.text: Early decelerations from fetal head compression
options.rationale: Early decelerations are shallow, symmetric, mirror the contraction and have no shoulders; the sharp drop within 20 seconds with shoulders described here is characteristic of variable, not early, decelerations (FIGO 2015; RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

options.text: Late decelerations from uteroplacental insufficiency
options.rationale: Late decelerations have a gradual onset (>30 seconds to nadir), nadir occurs after the peak of the contraction, and they return to baseline after the contraction ends; the sharp drop with recovery before the end of contraction here is not late (FIGO 2015).

options.text: Typical variable decelerations from umbilical cord compression
options.rationale: Sharp drop with nadir within 20-30 seconds, duration <60 seconds, shoulders (small accelerations either side), and recovery within the contraction are typical variables. The mechanism is umbilical cord compression triggering a baroreceptor-mediated vagal response. Typical variables with preserved variability and shoulders are not pathological (FIGO Intrapartum Fetal Monitoring 2015; RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

options.text: Atypical variable decelerations indicating fetal hypoxia
options.rationale: Atypical variables show loss of shoulders, slow return, biphasic shape, or duration >60 seconds; the description here retains shoulders and recovers within the contraction, making them typical not atypical (FIGO 2015).

options.text: Prolonged decelerations from acute fetal compromise
options.rationale: Prolonged decelerations last >3 minutes; these recover by the end of the contraction (under 60 seconds) so do not meet criteria (FIGO 2015).

explanation.summary: FIGO 2015 deceleration types: early (mirror contraction, gradual, head compression, benign); late (gradual, nadir after peak, uteroplacental insufficiency, concerning); variable (sharp, V-shaped, cord compression, typical vs atypical); prolonged (>3 min, acute compromise).
key_points: Early: gradual onset >=30 sec to nadir, mirrors contraction, no clinical action
Late: gradual onset, nadir after contraction peak, recovery after contraction ends, uteroplacental insufficiency
Variable typical: sharp, <60 sec, shoulders preserved, variability preserved, cord compression
Variable atypical: loss of shoulders, slow recovery, biphasic, duration >60 sec, overshoots without shoulders
Prolonged: >3 min; abruption, rupture, cord prolapse, hypotension
explanation.pearls: Shape and timing: gradual + late = uteroplacental; sharp + variable timing = cord. Shoulders are reassuring, overshoots without shoulders are not.

sources.label: NICE NG229 Intrapartum care for women at risk
NSW Health Maternity - Fetal heart rate monitoring

subtopic: Management of recurrent late decelerations with intrauterine resuscitation
stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 at 39+6 is in spontaneous labour, currently 7 cm dilated, with oxytocin augmentation at 8 mU/min for slow progress between 5 and 7 cm. Contractions are 5 in 10 lasting 60 seconds, palpate strong with no tachysystole. She has an epidural with good analgesia. Maternal BP 110/68, HR 88, T 36.8. Liquor clear, ARM 4 hours ago. The 36-week scan was unremarkable and GBS swab was negative. The CTG over the last 25 minutes shows a baseline of 150 bpm, variability of 5 to 8 bpm, no accelerations, and recurrent decelerations occurring with more than 50% of contractions. The decelerations begin gradually after the peak of each contraction, reach a nadir 60 seconds after the contraction peak and recover slowly after the contraction ends. There has been no prolonged deceleration and no vaginal bleeding. The obstetric registrar is at the bedside and theatre is available within 15 minutes.

data_table.CTG: Baseline 150, variability 5-8, no accelerations, recurrent late decelerations with >50% of contractions for 25 min.
data_table.Contractions: 5 in 10, 60 sec, strong on palpation. Oxytocin 8 mU/min.
data_table.Examination: 7 cm, +0 station, direct OA, clear liquor, no PV bleeding.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Continue oxytocin at the current rate and repeat assessment in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability are pathological; continuing oxytocin worsens fetal acidaemia (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

options.text: Proceed immediately to category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: Category 1 CS is for acute decompensation such as prolonged bradycardia, abruption or cord prolapse; a pathological trace without acute decompensation warrants intrauterine resuscitation and scalp lactate first when feasible (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

options.text: Increase oxytocin to expedite delivery before fetal compromise worsens
options.rationale: Increasing oxytocin in the face of a pathological trace is contraindicated and would worsen uteroplacental hypoperfusion (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

options.text: Stop oxytocin, reposition to left lateral, give 500 mL IV crystalloid, exclude reversible causes and consider fetal scalp lactate
options.rationale: Recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability indicate uteroplacental insufficiency and warrant intrauterine resuscitation (stop oxytocin, left lateral position, IV fluid bolus, exclude hypotension and tachysystole) followed by fetal scalp blood sampling for lactate to guide ongoing management (RANZCOG IFSM 2019; FIGO Intrapartum Fetal Monitoring 2015; NSW Health Maternity).
source_refs: NSW Health Maternity - Fetal heart rate monitoring

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 microg subcutaneously for tocolysis
options.rationale: Acute tocolysis is reserved for tachysystole-related compromise; contractions are 5 in 10 (not tachysystolic, defined as >5 in 10 averaged over 30 min) so tocolysis is not first-line (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).

explanation.summary: Late decelerations reflect uteroplacental insufficiency. Management: stop oxytocin, left lateral position, IV crystalloid bolus, correct maternal hypotension, exclude tachysystole (consider terbutaline if present), and perform fetal scalp blood sampling for lactate if the pattern persists.
key_points: Intrauterine resuscitation mnemonic: SPOILT (Stop oxytocin, Position, Oxygen no longer routinely recommended, IV fluid, Lower BP correct, Tocolysis if tachysystole)
Fetal scalp lactate: <4.2 normal, 4.2-4.7 borderline (repeat in 30 min), >=4.8 abnormal (deliver)
Reduced variability is the most predictive single feature of acidaemia
Tachysystole = >5 contractions in 10 minutes averaged over 30 min
Category 1 CS target decision-to-delivery 30 minutes
explanation.pearls: Late + flat = act. But act in the right order: resuscitate first, then sample, then deliver if not corrected.

sources.label: NSW Health Maternity - Fetal heart rate monitoring

subtopic: Management of a tight nuchal cord at delivery of the head
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 at 40+0 is in the active second stage at full dilatation following a 25-minute push, with an epidural providing good analgesia. The previous birth was an uncomplicated SVD. The fetus is cephalic with normal growth, and the GBS swab was negative. The CTG shortly before crowning showed a baseline of 140 bpm, variability 10, and mild variable decelerations recovering well. Liquor is clear. The fetal head crowns and is delivered slowly with perineal support. A cord is palpated wrapped tightly around the neck. An attempt to slip the cord over the head fails because it is too tight and the loop cannot be loosened either way. The shoulders have not yet delivered and the head is in restitution to LOT. No shoulder dystocia is recognised and the woman is pushing effectively with the next contraction expected within 30 to 60 seconds. The midwife asks the obstetric registrar what to do.

data_table.Examination: Head delivered, restituting to LOT. Tight nuchal cord, cannot be slipped over the head. No shoulder dystocia recognised.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Double-clamp and divide the cord immediately before delivery of the shoulders
options.rationale: Routine clamp-and-cut of a tight nuchal cord before shoulder delivery is no longer recommended because it converts a contained fetal-placental circulation into an acutely interrupted one if shoulder dystocia ensues, increasing the risk of hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy; the somersault manoeuvre is preferred (RANZCOG; NICE; Schorn 2014).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Apply firm traction to the fetal head to deliver the shoulders quickly before the cord tightens further
options.rationale: Forceful traction risks brachial plexus injury and does not address the cord; controlled delivery with the somersault is the correct approach (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Ask the woman to stop pushing and prepare for an emergency caesarean section
options.rationale: The head is delivered; caesarean is not feasible or indicated. The remaining task is to deliver the body safely, which the somersault accomplishes (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Cut the cord between two clamps only if shoulder dystocia is diagnosed
options.rationale: Cutting the cord during shoulder dystocia is particularly hazardous because it removes the fetoplacental reservoir while the fetus is wedged; the somersault should be used pre-emptively to avoid this scenario (RANZCOG; ACOG shoulder dystocia).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Perform the somersault manoeuvre - keep the head close to the maternal thigh as the body delivers, then unwind the cord
options.rationale: When a nuchal cord is too tight to slip over the head, the somersault manoeuvre (flexing the fetal head towards the maternal thigh while the body delivers in a forward roll) keeps the cord intact and the fetoplacental circulation preserved, then the loop is unwound after delivery. This is preferred over clamp-and-cut, which risks fetal hypovolaemia and HIE if shoulder dystocia complicates delivery (RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017; Schorn and Blanco 2014).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017
Schorn MN, Blanco JD 2014 Somersault manoeuvre

explanation.summary: Nuchal cord occurs in ~25% of deliveries. If loose: slip over head. If tight and cannot be slipped: somersault manoeuvre (flex head to maternal thigh, deliver body in forward roll, then unwind). Avoid routine clamp-and-cut before shoulder delivery.
key_points: Loose nuchal cord: slip over head, deliver normally
Tight nuchal cord: somersault manoeuvre preserves cord integrity
Clamp-and-cut before shoulder delivery is no longer recommended
If shoulder dystocia complicates and the cord has been cut, fetal hypovolaemia and HIE risk rise sharply
Nuchal cord is rarely the cause of poor neonatal outcome on its own
explanation.pearls: Somersault first, snip last. If you cut the cord and then meet a shoulder dystocia, the baby is on a clock without circulation.

sources.label: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017
Schorn MN, Blanco JD 2014 Somersault manoeuvre
KEMH Clinical Guidelines - Labour and Birth

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 40+2 is at full dilatation with the head at +3 station, direct OA, distending the perineum, after 90 minutes of pushing with an epidural. CTG has developed recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability for 12 minutes and the consultant has decided to perform a non-rotational forceps delivery in the room with theatre on standby. The bladder is empty, the epidural is topped up, consent has been obtained and a paediatric team has been notified. EFW 3500 g, pelvis gynaecoid, head fully engaged. The operator is preparing to apply Neville-Barnes forceps and the midwife asks whether an episiotomy should be performed and how it should be cut to minimise the risk of obstetric anal sphincter injury.

data_table.Examination: Full dilatation, +3, direct OA, head distending perineum, no abdominal palpable head.
data_table.Indication: Non-rotational forceps for pathological CTG at +3.
data_table.Maternal: Epidural topped up, bladder empty, consent obtained, BMI 26.

lead_in: Which best describes the appropriate episiotomy practice in this scenario?

options.text: Routine midline episiotomy from the fourchette in the 12 o'clock direction
options.rationale: Midline episiotomy is associated with increased risk of extension into the anal sphincter (OASI); mediolateral is preferred in Australian and UK practice (RANZCOG OASI 2020; RCOG).

options.text: Mediolateral episiotomy cut prior to crowning to allow descent
options.rationale: Episiotomy should be cut at crowning when the perineum is thinned; cutting before crowning increases blood loss and pain without benefit (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

options.text: Episiotomy is not indicated routinely for forceps delivery and should be avoided
options.rationale: Routine episiotomy in spontaneous birth is not recommended, but mediolateral episiotomy is specifically recommended for forceps delivery to reduce OASI (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: J-shaped episiotomy starting midline and curving laterally
options.rationale: J-shaped episiotomy is not standard practice in Australia; the recommended technique is mediolateral at 60 degrees (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

options.text: Mediolateral episiotomy from the fourchette at 60 degrees from the midline at crowning, given operative vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Mediolateral episiotomy cut at the time of crowning, originating at the fourchette and angled 60 degrees from the midline (right-sided for a right-handed operator), reduces the risk of obstetric anal sphincter injury during operative vaginal delivery. Episiotomy is not routine in spontaneous birth but is recommended for forceps and considered for ventouse (RANZCOG OASI 2020; RCOG Green-top 26 Operative Vaginal Birth).

explanation.summary: Episiotomy: not routine in spontaneous vaginal birth but indicated for operative vaginal delivery (particularly forceps), suspected imminent OASI, and some cases of shoulder dystocia. Mediolateral technique at 60 degrees from midline, cut at crowning, originating at the fourchette.
key_points: Mediolateral 60 degrees preferred over midline in Australia/UK
Midline episiotomy is standard in North America but has higher OASI rates
Cut at crowning, not before
Right-handed operators cut to the right; aim away from the anus
Episiotomy reduces OASI in forceps deliveries (NNT ~12 in nulliparas)
explanation.pearls: Sixty degrees to the side, at the fourchette, at crowning. Routine for forceps, considered for ventouse, never routine for spontaneous birth.

subtopic: Perineal trauma classification and OASI repair principles
stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 at 40+4 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth of a 4100 g infant with a mediolateral episiotomy following a 90-minute active second stage. The CTG had been normal throughout and Apgar scores were 9 and 9. Her booking BMI was 24, she has no medical comorbidities, GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative, and the antenatal course was uncomplicated apart from a borderline GTT that was normal on dietary management. Active management of the third stage has been completed with delivery of a complete placenta and intact membranes, with an estimated blood loss of 350 mL. On systematic per-vaginum and per-rectum examination by the obstetric registrar, there is a tear extending through the perineal skin, all layers of the perineal muscles, and approximately 60% of the thickness of the external anal sphincter complex. The internal anal sphincter is identified as intact on PR examination, and the rectal mucosa is intact with no buttonhole tear. Bleeding is controlled with pressure, the woman is haemodynamically stable, and she has a working epidural that has been topped up. The registrar plans repair in theatre with appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis and postoperative care.

data_table.Examination: Tear through perineal skin, perineal muscles, partial external anal sphincter (~60% thickness). Internal anal sphincter intact on PR. Rectal mucosa intact, no buttonhole.
data_table.Maternal: BP 116/72, HR 84, working epidural, haemodynamically stable.

lead_in: Which classification and repair plan is correct?

options.text: Second-degree tear, repair on the labour ward with absorbable suture
options.rationale: Second-degree tears involve perineal muscle but not the anal sphincter; involvement of the external anal sphincter makes this a third-degree (OASI) tear (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

options.text: Third-degree tear grade 3a, repair in theatre with end-to-end or overlap external anal sphincter repair
options.rationale: 3a is defined as <50% thickness of the external anal sphincter torn; this tear involves ~60% thickness, making it 3b (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

options.text: Third-degree tear grade 3c, repair in theatre with separate internal anal sphincter repair
options.rationale: 3c requires internal anal sphincter involvement; the internal sphincter is intact on PR in this scenario (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

options.text: Third-degree tear grade 3b, repair in theatre with end-to-end or overlap external anal sphincter repair, antibiotics, laxatives, physio follow-up
options.rationale: Sultan classification: 3a <50% EAS thickness, 3b >50% EAS thickness, 3c EAS and internal anal sphincter torn, 4 includes rectal mucosa. With ~60% EAS thickness and intact IAS and mucosa, this is a 3b. Repair in theatre under regional or general anaesthesia with end-to-end or overlap technique for the EAS (equivalent outcomes), single-dose IV antibiotics, oral laxatives (lactulose for 10-14 days), pelvic floor physiotherapy, and 6-12 week follow-up (RANZCOG OASI 2020; RCOG Green-top 29 The Management of Third- and Fourth-degree Perineal Tears).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top 29 The Management of Third- and Fourth-degree Perineal Tears

options.text: Fourth-degree tear, repair in theatre with rectal mucosal closure first
options.rationale: Fourth-degree tears extend through the rectal mucosa; the mucosa is intact here (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

explanation.summary: Sultan classification of perineal trauma: 1st - skin only; 2nd - perineal muscle; 3rd - anal sphincter (3a <50% EAS, 3b >50% EAS, 3c EAS+IAS); 4th - rectal mucosa. OASI repair: theatre, regional anaesthesia, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, end-to-end or overlap EAS repair (equivalent outcomes), separate IAS repair if torn, mucosal repair for 4th degree, laxatives 10-14 days, physiotherapy, follow-up 6-12 weeks.
key_points: 3a <50% EAS, 3b >50% EAS, 3c EAS + IAS torn, 4th involves rectal mucosa
Repair in theatre with good lighting and assistance
PR examination is mandatory after every vaginal delivery to exclude buttonhole tear
Single-dose IV broad-spectrum antibiotics at repair, then oral 5-7 days
Lactulose (not stool softener) to reduce wound dehiscence
Future deliveries: vaginal birth acceptable if asymptomatic; counsel about 5-7% recurrence
explanation.pearls: PR every birth. If you do not feel the sphincter, you cannot exclude OASI. The 50% rule separates 3a from 3b.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top 29 The Management of Third- and Fourth-degree Perineal Tears

subtopic: Active management of the third stage of labour
stem: A 33-year-old G3P2 at 39+5 has just had an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth of a 3500 g infant after a 6-hour active labour. The baby is on the maternal chest with vigorous cry and good tone, Apgar 9 at 1 minute. Her previous two births were vaginal at term with normal third stages and no PPH, and her booking haemoglobin was 122 g/L. There are no risk factors for postpartum haemorrhage: no prolonged labour, no induction or augmentation with oxytocin, no chorioamnionitis, no polyhydramnios and no antepartum haemorrhage. Active management of the third stage is planned as per unit protocol. Maternal observations are stable, the uterus is palpable at the umbilicus and the cord has been clamped and cut after 90 seconds of delayed clamping. The medical student is asked by the midwife to outline the three components of active management as recommended in Australia by RANZCOG and to explain why active management is preferred over physiological management.

data_table.Maternal: BP 118/74, HR 88, EBL so far 200 mL, uterus palpable at umbilicus.
data_table.Neonate: Apgar 9 at 1 min, on maternal chest, delayed cord clamping at 90 sec.
data_table.Risk factors for PPH: None: parity 3, no APH, no prolonged labour, no induction, no chorioamnionitis.

lead_in: Which best describes the three components of active management of the third stage?

options.text: Oxytocin 10 IU IM with delivery of the anterior shoulder (or shortly after birth), controlled cord traction with counter-traction on the uterus, and uterine massage after placental delivery
options.rationale: Active management of the third stage comprises three components: prophylactic uterotonic (oxytocin 10 IU IM with the anterior shoulder or shortly after birth), controlled cord traction (Brandt-Andrews) with suprapubic counter-traction to prevent uterine inversion, and uterine massage after placental delivery to maintain tone. Active management reduces PPH by approximately 60% compared with physiological management and is standard in Australia (RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017; WHO PPH prevention 2012; NICE NG235).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Oxytocin 5 IU IV at delivery, immediate cord clamping, manual placental removal
options.rationale: Oxytocin dose is 10 IU IM (not 5 IU IV), immediate cord clamping is no longer recommended (delay 1-3 min unless neonatal compromise), and manual removal is reserved for retained placenta (RANZCOG; WHO; NICE NG235).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Ergometrine 500 microg IM at delivery, cord clamping at placental separation, and bimanual compression
options.rationale: Ergometrine alone is not first-line prophylaxis (it is contraindicated in hypertension and PE); oxytocin is preferred. Bimanual compression is a treatment for PPH, not prophylaxis (RANZCOG; AMH).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Carbetocin 100 microg IV, delayed cord clamping, and inspection of the placenta
options.rationale: Carbetocin is an alternative to oxytocin (particularly at caesarean), but the three components of active management as defined in guidelines are uterotonic + controlled cord traction + uterine massage. Placental inspection is routine but not one of the named components (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Misoprostol 600 microg oral, cord milking, and uterine massage
options.rationale: Misoprostol is a backup uterotonic in low-resource settings or oxytocin shortage; oxytocin IM is first-line in Australia (RANZCOG; WHO).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

explanation.summary: Active management of third stage: 1) oxytocin 10 IU IM with anterior shoulder (or after birth); 2) controlled cord traction (Brandt-Andrews) with suprapubic counter-pressure; 3) uterine massage after placental delivery. Reduces PPH ~60%. Delayed cord clamping (1-3 min) is now incorporated.
key_points: Oxytocin 10 IU IM is first-line; carbetocin 100 microg IM/IV alternative
Ergometrine 500 microg IM avoided in hypertension/PE
Syntometrine (oxytocin 5 IU + ergometrine 500 microg) increases nausea/hypertension and is second-line
Delayed cord clamping 1-3 min unless asphyxia or maternal indication
Active management reduces PPH from ~13% to ~5% (Begley Cochrane 2019)
explanation.pearls: Three components: drug, traction, massage. Memorise the order; it is exam-favoured.

sources.label: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

subtopic: Retained placenta - timing and approach to manual removal
stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 40+1 has had a spontaneous vaginal birth of a 3400 g infant 45 minutes ago after a 7-hour labour with no oxytocin augmentation and no instrumental delivery. Active management of the third stage was performed with oxytocin 10 IU IM at delivery of the anterior shoulder, and controlled cord traction has been attempted multiple times by the experienced midwife and registrar without placental delivery. There is no significant ongoing bleeding, total EBL is 350 mL. The uterus is palpable and contracted at the umbilicus and there is no apparent uterine inversion. The cord is intact, appears at the introitus, and shows no signs of having broken on traction. The woman has an epidural in situ that is providing good analgesia and remains haemodynamically stable. Her previous birth was an uncomplicated SVD with a normal third stage 3 years ago. She had one D&C for a missed miscarriage at 10 weeks two years ago and an LLETZ for CIN2 four years ago. No previous caesarean. The placenta is therefore retained at 45 minutes despite active management with no third-stage haemorrhage. The midwife asks the obstetric registrar what should happen next, and the on-call consultant has been informed.

data_table.Maternal: BP 118/72, HR 86, EBL 350 mL, uterus contracted at umbilicus.
data_table.Third stage: 45 min, oxytocin 10 IU IM given, controlled cord traction unsuccessful.
data_table.Past history: One vaginal birth, one D&C for missed miscarriage, one LLETZ for CIN2.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue expectant management for a further 30 minutes given no significant bleeding
options.rationale: Retained placenta at 30 minutes with active management is the trigger for definitive intervention; ongoing delay risks PPH and increases the threshold for intervention without benefit (RANZCOG; NICE NG235).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Inject 20 IU oxytocin in 20 mL saline into the umbilical vein
options.rationale: Umbilical vein oxytocin injection has been studied but Cochrane and RANZCOG do not recommend it as standard practice; manual removal in theatre is the recommended next step (Cochrane 2011; RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Administer ergometrine 500 microg IM and reattempt controlled cord traction
options.rationale: Ergometrine causes tetanic uterine contraction which traps rather than expels the placenta and is contraindicated for retained placenta; it also worsens hypertension (RANZCOG; AMH).

options.text: Perform manual removal at the bedside without anaesthesia given the woman has an epidural
options.rationale: Even with an epidural, manual removal should occur in theatre with full surgical readiness because of the risk of significant haemorrhage, uterine inversion and the possibility of accreta requiring escalation (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017

options.text: Transfer to theatre for manual removal of the placenta under regional or general anaesthesia, with IV antibiotic prophylaxis and an oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: Retained placenta with active management at >=30 minutes requires manual removal in theatre under regional (or general if regional not feasible) anaesthesia. Manage with IV access (two large-bore cannulae, FBC and crossmatch), single-dose IV broad-spectrum antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g. cefazolin and metronidazole or co-amoxiclav), oxytocin infusion during and after removal, and assessment for placenta accreta spectrum particularly with a history of uterine instrumentation (RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017; NICE NG235; Royal Women's Hospital Retained Placenta guideline).
source_refs: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017
Royal Women's Hospital Retained Placenta guideline

explanation.summary: Retained placenta: >=30 min with active management or >=60 min with physiological management. Move to theatre for manual removal under regional or general anaesthesia. IV access, group and hold/crossmatch, IV antibiotics, oxytocin infusion. Suspect placenta accreta if removal is difficult or in women with previous CS, uterine surgery, or instrumentation. PPH and infection are the main complications.
key_points: Active management threshold: 30 min
Physiological management threshold: 60 min
Theatre, regional anaesthesia preferred, single-dose IV broad-spectrum antibiotics
Oxytocin infusion during and after manual removal
Accreta risk: previous CS, D&C, myomectomy, placenta praevia - have escalation pathway ready
explanation.pearls: 30 minutes with active management means theatre, not another bedside pull. Always check for accreta clues in the history.

sources.label: RANZCOG Provision of routine intrapartum care 2017
Royal Women's Hospital Retained Placenta guideline
Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotics for obstetric procedures

subtopic: Pre-eclampsia at term - timing of delivery and intrapartum management
stem: A 34-year-old G1P0 at 37+3 attends day assessment with a one-week history of frontal headaches not relieved by paracetamol and new BP elevation noted by her GP. Her booking BP was 110/68 and her booking BMI was 28, with no chronic hypertension, no renal disease, no autoimmune disease, no prior gestational hypertension and no family history of pre-eclampsia. She is a non-smoker and took aspirin 100 mg from 12 weeks. Today her BP on arrival is 158/102 on repeat measurement 15 minutes apart in the seated position with an appropriately sized cuff, urine protein creatinine ratio is 65 mg/mmol, platelets are 145 x10^9/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, ALT 32 U/L, urate 0.39 mmol/L, and the fetus is appropriately grown on the 40th centile with normal umbilical artery Dopplers, normal AFI and a reassuring CTG. She has had labetalol 200 mg orally with a fall to 142/92 after one hour and the headache has eased. There is no eclampsia, no epigastric pain, no visual disturbance, no clonus, no pulmonary oedema and no oliguria. On vaginal examination the cervix is closed, posterior, 2 cm long, firm, station -3, giving a Bishop score of 1. The on-call consultant reviews her in the unit and discusses options regarding admission, antihypertensive management, MgSO4 use and timing and route of delivery.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 158/102 then 142/92 post-labetalol, HR 88, T 36.8, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Bloods: Platelets 145 x10^9/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, ALT 32 U/L, urate 0.39 mmol/L. uPCR 65 mg/mmol.
data_table.Fetal: 40th centile, normal Dopplers, normal AFI, reassuring CTG.
data_table.Cervix: Closed, posterior, 2 cm long, firm, station -3 (Bishop 1).

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Discharge home on oral labetalol with weekly review until 39+0 then induce labour
options.rationale: Pre-eclampsia diagnosed at or beyond 37+0 weeks is an indication for delivery in the same admission, not expectant management to 39 weeks (SOMANZ 2023; NICE NG133).

options.text: Admit, continue oral antihypertensive to keep BP <140/90, plan induction of labour in this admission, give MgSO4 only if severe features develop
options.rationale: Pre-eclampsia at >=37+0 weeks is an indication for delivery (SOMANZ 2023; NICE NG133; HYPITAT trial 2009). With a Bishop score of 1 the route is induction of labour rather than caesarean unless other obstetric indications. Antihypertensive target is <140/90 (oral labetalol, nifedipine or methyldopa). MgSO4 for seizure prophylaxis is indicated for severe pre-eclampsia (severe hypertension >=160/110, neurological features, severe biochemical features) rather than routine non-severe disease (SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023; NICE NG133).

options.text: Proceed to category 2 caesarean section today given the unfavourable cervix
options.rationale: Bishop 1 does not mandate caesarean in non-severe PE with reassuring fetal status; ripening with Foley or PGE2 followed by induction is appropriate (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).

options.text: Admit, give MgSO4 4 g loading and 1 g/h infusion regardless of severity, and induce labour
options.rationale: Routine MgSO4 for all PE is not recommended in non-severe disease in SOMANZ; it is reserved for severe PE or eclampsia or imminent eclampsia features (severe BP, neurological signs, severe biochemistry) (SOMANZ 2023).

options.text: Admit for 48 hours of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM x 2 doses before induction
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids are not indicated at 37+3 weeks because fetal lung maturity is sufficiently advanced; steroids are used between 24+0 and 34+6 (RANZCOG corticosteroids; SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Pre-eclampsia at >=37+0 weeks: deliver. Route depends on Bishop and other obstetric factors. Antihypertensive target <140/90 (oral labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa; IV hydralazine or labetalol for severe). MgSO4 for severe PE (4 g IV loading, 1 g/h infusion) for seizure prophylaxis. Continue antihypertensives postpartum and review at 6 weeks.
key_points: PE definition: new HTN >=20 wks plus one of proteinuria, AKI, hepatic, neurological, haematological, uteroplacental dysfunction
Severe features: BP >=160/110, platelets <100, creatinine >90, ALT >2x ULN, headache/visual/clonus, pulmonary oedema, FGR
Delivery: >=37+0 PE deliver; 34-37 expectant in selected cases with severe features under tertiary care; <34 expectant unless severe and uncontrolled
MgSO4: 4 g IV over 20 min then 1 g/h for 24 h post-birth or post-last seizure
Postpartum BP typically peaks day 3-5
explanation.pearls: 37+0 PE = deliver. MgSO4 is for severe, not all. Antihypertensive target is <140/90 antenatally and postnatally.

subtopic: Intrapartum GBS prophylaxis - regimen and penicillin allergy
stem: A 27-year-old G2P1 at 39+0 attends in spontaneous labour at 5 cm. Her vaginorectal GBS screening swab at 36+2 was positive. Her previous birth was an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery at 39+5 and the infant had no early-onset GBS sepsis and was discharged home well. She has a documented allergy to penicillin recorded in detail in her record as widespread urticarial rash starting 2 days into a course of amoxicillin for a dental infection three years ago, without angioedema, without wheeze, without hypotension and without anaphylaxis or hospitalisation. Susceptibility testing on the GBS isolate from this pregnancy's swab is documented in the pathology report as susceptible to clindamycin and erythromycin. She has no other comorbidities, is afebrile, has a reassuring CTG, normal observations and no other risk factors for intrapartum infection. Membranes ruptured 30 minutes ago with clear liquor and CTG is reassuring with baseline 138, variability 10-15, accelerations and no decelerations. The midwife asks the resident which antibiotic regimen should be commenced now for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis.

data_table.Microbiology: GBS positive (vaginorectal swab 36+2). Isolate susceptible to clindamycin and erythromycin.
data_table.Allergy: Penicillin - widespread urticarial rash, no angioedema/wheeze/hypotension (non-severe, non-anaphylactic).
data_table.Labour: 5 cm, SROM 30 min ago, clear liquor, reassuring CTG.
data_table.Other risk factors: None: afebrile, no prolonged ROM, no preterm labour.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic regimen for GBS prophylaxis?

options.text: Benzylpenicillin 3 g (5 MU) IV loading then 1.8 g (3 MU) IV every 4 hours until delivery
options.rationale: Although first-line for non-allergic women, she has a documented non-severe penicillin allergy. Severity matters: with non-severe (non-anaphylactic) rash, cefazolin is appropriate; with severe/anaphylactic allergy, clindamycin if susceptible (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG; CDC GBS 2019).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Clindamycin 600 mg IV every 8 hours until delivery
options.rationale: Clindamycin is reserved for severe (anaphylactic) penicillin allergy with documented isolate susceptibility; this woman has non-severe allergy where cefazolin is preferred (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG GBS 2019).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Vancomycin 20 mg/kg IV every 8 hours until delivery
options.rationale: Vancomycin is third-line, used only when there is severe penicillin allergy and clindamycin resistance or unknown susceptibility (Therapeutic Guidelines; CDC).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Cefazolin 2 g IV loading then 1 g IV every 8 hours until delivery
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines Australia and RANZCOG recommend cefazolin for women with non-severe (non-anaphylactic) penicillin allergy because cross-reactivity with first-generation cephalosporins is low (<2%). Loading dose 2 g IV with a maintenance dose of 1 g IV every 8 hours until delivery. Clindamycin is reserved for severe (anaphylactic) allergy with isolate susceptibility (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic; RANZCOG Maternal Group B Streptococcus 2019; CDC GBS prevention 2019).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g 8-hourly until delivery
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not achieve reliable intrapartum levels for GBS prophylaxis; IV route is mandatory. Amoxicillin is also a penicillin and contraindicated in penicillin allergy (Therapeutic Guidelines).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

explanation.summary: Intrapartum GBS prophylaxis: benzylpenicillin 3 g IV load then 1.8 g IV q4h until birth (first-line). Non-severe penicillin allergy: cefazolin 2 g IV load then 1 g IV q8h. Severe (anaphylactic) penicillin allergy: clindamycin 600 mg IV q8h if susceptible, vancomycin 20 mg/kg IV q8h if not. Adequate prophylaxis = >=4 h before birth.
key_points: Australian screening: vaginorectal swab at 35-37 weeks OR risk-based (preterm, prolonged ROM, intrapartum fever, previous infant with GBS disease, GBS bacteriuria this pregnancy)
Severe allergy = anaphylaxis, angioedema, respiratory distress, hypotension; non-severe = rash without systemic features
Cefazolin is safe in non-severe penicillin allergy (cross-reactivity <2%)
Clindamycin resistance varies 13-20%; always check susceptibility
Adequate prophylaxis is >=4 h before birth
explanation.pearls: Allergy severity drives choice: non-severe = cefazolin, severe + susceptible = clindamycin, severe + resistant/unknown = vancomycin.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

subtopic: Term PROM - timing of induction versus expectant management
stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 39+4 presents at 0200 with a sudden gush of clear fluid from the vagina that occurred 30 minutes ago while watching television. On sterile speculum examination there is a pool of clear amniotic fluid in the posterior fornix, with a positive fern test on a dried slide and a positive IGFBP-1 swab. There is no offensive odour, no PV bleeding, no meconium and no fetal scalp visible. The fetus is cephalic, the head is engaged on abdominal palpation, and the CTG is reassuring with a baseline of 138 bpm, good variability of 10-15 bpm, accelerations and no decelerations. The cervix is closed and 2 cm long on sterile speculum visualisation; digital examination has not been performed. She is afebrile at T 36.7, HR 78, BP 116/72 and her white cell count is 9 x10^9/L with CRP <5 mg/L. Her booking BMI was 24, she has no medical comorbidities and her GBS vaginorectal swab at 36+1 was negative. She is having occasional irregular tightenings every 10-20 minutes but is not in established labour. She would prefer to avoid medical interventions if possible and is keen on as physiological a birth as is safe. The on-call obstetric registrar discusses her options.

data_table.Examination: SROM confirmed (pool, ferning, IGFBP-1 positive), clear liquor, no meconium, no foul odour, head engaged.

lead_in: Which best describes the optimal management plan for this woman?

options.text: Discharge home with daily review for up to 72 hours, then induce if not in labour
options.rationale: Australian practice for term PROM does not support prolonged outpatient expectant management beyond 24 hours; chorioamnionitis risk rises with time and induction within 24 h reduces this risk (Hannah TermPROM; RANZCOG; NICE NG235).
source_refs: RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Offer immediate induction of labour with oxytocin or expectant management for up to 24 hours, with induction at 24 hours if not in labour; commence intrapartum GBS prophylaxis only if ROM exceeds 18 hours or other risk factors develop
options.rationale: The Hannah TermPROM trial (NEJM 1996) established that immediate IOL with oxytocin or expectant management for up to 96 hours have similar perinatal outcomes, but immediate IOL reduces chorioamnionitis and postpartum endometritis. Current Australian practice offers women either immediate IOL or expectant management with a clear plan to induce by approximately 24 hours from rupture, balancing maternal preference with infection risk. With negative GBS, intrapartum antibiotics are based on risk factors (ROM >18 h, intrapartum fever) rather than routine (Hannah ME et al NEJM 1996; RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes; NICE NG235).
source_refs: RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Commence empirical IV ampicillin and gentamicin immediately and induce labour with prostaglandin gel
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not indicated in the absence of clinical infection or risk factors (GBS unknown/positive, prolonged ROM, fever); they would be added if she developed chorioamnionitis or ROM >18 h with negative GBS (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform a digital vaginal examination to assess Bishop score and plan immediate caesarean section
options.rationale: Digital vaginal examination after PROM should be deferred until labour is established or induction is planned because each exam increases infection risk; PROM is not an indication for caesarean (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Commence vaginal prostaglandin gel for cervical ripening overnight regardless of preference
options.rationale: Patient preference matters and should be incorporated into the plan; immediate ripening is one option but not the sole appropriate plan (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

explanation.summary: Term PROM: offer immediate IOL or expectant management up to ~24 h. The Hannah TermPROM trial showed equivalent perinatal outcomes between immediate IOL and expectant management, with lower chorioamnionitis and endometritis with immediate IOL. Avoid digital VE until necessary. Intrapartum GBS prophylaxis if GBS positive or ROM >18 h with unknown GBS, or other risk factors.
key_points: Confirm PROM: pool, ferning, IGFBP-1 or PAMG-1 if uncertain
Hannah TermPROM: immediate IOL reduces chorioamnionitis (4% vs 8.6%) and endometritis without increasing CS
Avoid digital VE until established labour or induction
GBS-negative + ROM <18 h + no fever = no antibiotics
GBS-positive = intrapartum prophylaxis as soon as labour or IOL begins
ROM >18 h with unknown GBS = treat as positive
explanation.pearls: PROM at term is a shared decision: immediate IOL versus brief expectant. Avoid digital exams; they buy infection without information.

sources.label: RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

subtopic: Operative vaginal delivery - prerequisites and failed forceps disposition
stem: A 32-year-old G1P0 at 40+0 has been pushing actively in the second stage for 110 minutes with an epidural providing adequate sensory block. Her booking BMI was 27, she has no medical comorbidities, the GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative and her 36-week scan showed an appropriately grown fetus on the 55th centile with an EFW of 3500 g, normal Dopplers and normal liquor. The fetal head is at +2 station, in direct OA, with no caput and no moulding on examination by the registrar. The CTG has developed recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability for 8 minutes, and the registrar wishes to expedite delivery. The bladder has been catheterised and emptied, the maternal pelvis is clinically gynaecoid, and verbal consent for instrumental delivery in the room with theatre on standby has been obtained from the woman. Adequate epidural analgesia has been confirmed with cold testing, the paediatric team has been notified and the anaesthetist is in the room. The registrar applies Neville-Barnes forceps correctly with the operator's posterior wrist supported and performs three pulls with descent of the head from +2 to +3, and then no further descent on the fourth pull despite correct application, correct axis traction, and an effective maternal push with a contraction. The fetal head has not yet crowned and the perineum is not yet stretched. The consultant is now at the bedside and reviews the scenario before deciding the next step.

data_table.Examination: Head +3 after three pulls, no further descent on fourth pull, direct OA, no caput/moulding initially.
data_table.CTG: Recurrent late decelerations, reduced variability, ongoing 8 min.
data_table.Maternal: Adequate epidural, bladder catheterised, consent obtained, theatre on standby, gynaecoid pelvis.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reapply forceps with a different cup angle and attempt two further pulls
options.rationale: Repeated forceps attempts after failure to descend with three correct pulls is associated with significant maternal and neonatal trauma and is not recommended; transition to caesarean is required (RANZCOG operative vaginal birth; RCOG Green-top 26).

options.text: Switch to a ventouse to complete delivery given the head is at +3
options.rationale: Sequential use of instruments (forceps then ventouse or vice versa) is associated with significantly increased neonatal morbidity (intracranial haemorrhage, subgaleal haemorrhage, cephalhaematoma) and should be avoided. Failed forceps mandates caesarean rather than a second instrument (RANZCOG; RCOG Green-top 26; ACOG).

options.text: Transfer immediately to theatre for category 1 caesarean section with senior consultant present
options.rationale: Failed forceps with pathological CTG mandates category 1 caesarean section. Sequential use of instruments is contraindicated due to combined neonatal trauma risk. The trial of instrumental should occur with theatre on standby precisely so this conversion can occur within minutes. The senior consultant should be present given the complexity (RANZCOG Instrumental Vaginal Birth 2016; RCOG Green-top 26 Operative Vaginal Birth; NICE NG235).

options.text: Ask the mother to push without the forceps for one more contraction
options.rationale: The CTG is pathological and pushing without instrument has already failed for >100 minutes; expediting delivery in theatre is required (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform a symphysiotomy to widen the pelvis
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is reserved for cases of shoulder dystocia or where caesarean is impossible (low-resource settings) and is not appropriate here (RANZCOG; WHO).

explanation.summary: Operative vaginal delivery prerequisites: full dilatation, ruptured membranes, head engaged (no abdominal palpable), station >=0 (preferably +1 or below), known position, adequate analgesia, empty bladder, consent, appropriate operator. Failure: no descent with three correct pulls, or non-completion after a reasonable trial. Failed forceps requires caesarean - do NOT switch instruments.
key_points: Forceps: better for OP/OT after rotation (Kielland) and where pulling is needed
Ventouse: better tolerated maternally, fewer perineal injuries, but higher failure rate, contraindicated <34 weeks and face presentation
Sequential instruments: significantly higher neonatal morbidity - avoid
Trial of instrumental in theatre when high failure risk (BMI >30, EFW >4 kg, OP/OT, mid-cavity, prolonged second stage)
Category 1 CS decision-to-delivery interval target 30 minutes
explanation.pearls: If forceps fails, the next stop is theatre, not the ventouse. The trolley is on standby for a reason.

sources.label: Royal Women's Hospital Operative Vaginal Birth guideline

subtopic: Twin delivery - mode of birth with second twin non-cephalic
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 with a dichorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy at 37+5 is in spontaneous labour. Twin 1 is cephalic and growth-appropriate on the 45th centile, twin 2 has been transverse throughout the third trimester on serial scans with the back inferior and is 200 g smaller than twin 1 (35th centile, within the 20% inter-twin discordance threshold and with no growth discordance suggestive of selective FGR). She had a previous uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth at 39+1 of a singleton with a normal third stage and no perineal trauma beyond a second-degree tear. There is no previous caesarean, no uterine surgery and no medical comorbidities. Her booking BMI was 26, the GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative and she has taken iron supplementation throughout the pregnancy with a current haemoglobin of 118 g/L. Both fetuses have reassuring CTGs on dual monitoring. The cervix is 6 cm, station of the leading twin is -1, membranes are intact for the leading twin and ARM has not been performed, and an experienced obstetric consultant with extensive twin delivery experience is available throughout the labour. The anaesthetist is in the unit and an epidural has been sited and is providing good analgesia. She has expressed a clear preference for a vaginal birth if safe. After 4 hours she is fully dilated, twin 1 head is at +3, and she pushes for 25 minutes to deliver a 2900 g cephalic infant with normal Apgars. On bedside rescan by the consultant immediately after delivery of twin 1, twin 2 remains transverse with the back inferior. The CTG of twin 2 is reassuring at this point, membranes of twin 2 are intact, and the consultant is at the bedside in the delivery suite with a theatre and a paediatric team on standby.

data_table.Antenatal: DCDA twins, twin 1 cephalic, twin 2 transverse back-inferior. EFW concordance within 20%.
data_table.After twin 1 delivery: Twin 2 remains transverse, back inferior, reassuring CTG, intact membranes.
data_table.Operator: Experienced consultant at bedside, theatre available, paediatrics on standby.
data_table.Maternal: Epidural in situ, good analgesia, haemodynamically stable.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step for delivery of twin 2?

options.text: Internal podalic version followed by breech extraction of twin 2
options.rationale: With twin 2 transverse after delivery of twin 1 and an experienced operator available, internal podalic version (operator inserts a hand through the cervix, identifies a fetal foot, brings it down through the introitus) followed by breech extraction is the preferred approach in Australia and is supported by the Twin Birth Study (Barrett JF et al NEJM 2013). External cephalic version is an alternative but has lower success after delivery of the first twin and risks fetal distress and abruption. Combined vaginal-CS delivery has worse outcomes than planned CS. The reassuring CTG provides a window for intervention, and internal podalic version with breech extraction is the most reliable mode in experienced hands. CS should be reserved for failed manoeuvres or non-reassuring CTG (RANZCOG Management of Twin Pregnancy 2022; RCOG Green-top 51 Twin and Triplet Pregnancy).
source_refs: Barrett JF et al NEJM 2013 (Twin Birth Study)
RCOG Green-top 51 Twin and Triplet Pregnancy

options.text: External cephalic version under ultrasound, then expect spontaneous cephalic delivery
options.rationale: ECV after delivery of twin 1 is an option but has lower success rates than internal podalic version (~60% vs >90% in experienced hands), and any delay risks cord prolapse, abruption or cervical closure. ECV may be tried first by some operators but internal podalic version + breech extraction is more reliable when the operator is experienced (RANZCOG; Twin Birth Study).

options.text: Category 2 caesarean section for twin 2
options.rationale: Combined vaginal-caesarean delivery (vaginal twin 1, CS twin 2) is associated with worse outcomes for both mother and twin 2 and should be reserved for failed manoeuvres or fetal compromise; the Twin Birth Study demonstrated that planned vaginal birth in a centre with experienced operators is safe (Barrett NEJM 2013).
source_refs: Barrett JF et al NEJM 2013 (Twin Birth Study)

options.text: Await spontaneous descent of twin 2 for 60 minutes before any intervention
options.rationale: Twin 2 transverse will not descend spontaneously; the inter-twin interval should be as short as practical (commonly within 30 minutes) before intervention to avoid cervical closure, cord prolapse with subsequent ARM, and abruption (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform ARM of twin 2 membranes immediately to allow descent
options.rationale: ARM of twin 2 while transverse risks cord prolapse and limb presentation; membranes should be left intact until the presenting part is engaged after version or breech extraction (RANZCOG; RCOG).

explanation.summary: Twin delivery: twin 1 cephalic and twin 2 cephalic = planned vaginal birth. Twin 1 cephalic, twin 2 non-cephalic = vaginal birth is appropriate where an experienced operator is available; CS only if both non-cephalic or if specific contraindications. After delivery of twin 1 with non-cephalic twin 2: internal podalic version + breech extraction (preferred) or ECV if back-superior. ARM of twin 2 only after presenting part engaged. Inter-twin interval should be minimised.
key_points: Twin Birth Study (Barrett NEJM 2013): planned vaginal birth equivalent to planned CS for selected DCDA/MCDA at 32+0 to 38+6 with twin 1 cephalic
Twin 2 non-cephalic: internal podalic version + breech extraction is preferred
ECV alternative for transverse twin 2 with back superior
Monoamniotic twins always CS (cord entanglement risk)
Inter-twin interval typically <30 min to minimise cervical closure and abruption risk
Combined vaginal-CS delivery worse than planned CS for both
explanation.pearls: Twin 1 cephalic + experienced operator = trial of vaginal birth even if twin 2 is breech or transverse. The non-cephalic second twin is not by itself a CS indication.

sources.label: Barrett JF et al NEJM 2013 (Twin Birth Study)
RCOG Green-top 51 Twin and Triplet Pregnancy
NICE NG137 Twin and triplet pregnancy
Royal Women's Hospital Twin Pregnancy guideline
